Obesity remains a major global health concern, and psychosocial stressors such as burnout may contribute to its

Results
Burnout showed a strong and graded association with obesity across all indices. Compared with low burnout, high burnout
associated with up to a 40% higher odds of obesity by BMl, and even stronger associations when using CUN-BAE and
was
Figures and Tables
METS-VF. Women, older employees, and those from lower social classes were disproportionately affected. Adherence to a
Mediterranean
diet and engagement in regular physical activity were associated with lower obesity risk among participants
with higher burnout levels.
94,315 workers attended their occupational examination

Burnout is
significant and independent correlate of obesity in working populations, particularly when measured with
